title: Untitled
research_problem: Investigate pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and pedestrian perceptions toward different pedestrian facilities (signalized/unsignalized intersection crosswalks, midblock crosswalks, physical barriers, shelters, colored paving, warning signs) in a divided urban boulevard next to Michigan State University
method_summary: Combined observational study using video cameras (8 cameras covering 1km corridor) to record pedestrian movements and user survey (711 respondents) to assess pedestrian perceptions. Data collected on Grand River Avenue (M-43), East Lansing, MI, including spatial and temporal crossing compliance rates calculated from video analysis and survey responses
method_family: Empirical observational study with survey research
tasks: Pedestrian crossing compliance analysis; Pedestrian perception assessment; Factor analysis influencing crossing choices; Gender and age factor analysis on responses
datasets: Survey data from 711 pedestrians (daily and occasional users); Video observation data from 8 video cameras; Grand River Avenue study site: 1km divided boulevard section from Abbott to Bogue Streets, East Lansing, Michigan
metrics: Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) - percentage of pedestrians using designated crosswalk locations; Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) - percentage complying with pedestrian signal indication; Survey response rates and preference percentages
claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks showed the highest pedestrian preference (83% reported preference to cross) and high crossing compliance rate (71.2%)
limitations: Study site was limited to one specific urban corridor (Grand River Avenue) which may not be representative of all urban environments | Survey response rate was 17.9% (897 completed from 5000 distributed), which may introduce non-response bias | Study population primarily consisted of university affiliates (MSU), limiting generalizability to other populations | Data collection excluded rainy days to protect equipment, potentially missing pedestrian behavior under wet conditions | User preferences may not correlate highly with safety considerations - relationship between safety and pedestrian acceptance needs further analysis
notes: Study combines two data sources: behavioral observation via video cameras and user perception via surveys; Crosswalk Influence Area (CIA) and Crosswalk Area (CA) methodologies used to calculate compliance rates; Statistical tests showed no significant differences in responses by age or gender (except distance factor for elderly); Pedestrian warning signs at midblock locations were generally disapproved by pedestrians despite potential safety value; Signalized intersections were effective for pedestrian channelization but showed lower compliance with signal indication under low traffic demand; 68% of respondents agreed the study site is a safe corridor for pedestrians

title: Untitled
research_problem: Modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities, specifically addressing the high rate of pedestrian signal non-compliance (red light running) that differs significantly from pedestrians in developed countries.
method_summary: Developed a pedestrian micro-simulation model that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying and opportunistic. Used questionnaires (495 respondents in Xi'an) to determine proportions of each type under different circumstances, and extracted time gap distributions from videotape (989 samples) to determine gap acceptance criteria. The model incorporates external factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle flow.
tasks: pedestrian_red_light_running_simulation; gap_acceptance_modeling; traffic_violation_behavior_modeling
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (November 2003); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples, June 2003 to April 2004)
metrics: proportion_of_red_light_jumpers; proportion_of_violation_followers; time_gap_delta_T_distribution
claim_text: The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running in Chinese cities, with simulation results showing 45-83% red light violation rates
limitations: Simulation results initially deviated from field data due to questionnaire response bias and inability of respondents to represent all pedestrians accurately | The model only considers pedestrians' decision making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles; does not account for vehicle acceleration/deceleration changes | The calculation method of ΔT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical | The model may not fully capture the complex human decision-making process impacted by numerous factors in real traffic environments
notes: Published in Transportation Research Part A: Policy and Practice, Volume 40, Issue 3, March 2006. The study addresses a critical gap in traffic micro-simulation for developing countries, particularly China where pedestrian violation rates can reach 66.7% in certain road sections. The model may be applicable to other developing cities with similar traffic characteristics.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Pedestrian signal non-compliance (red light running) in Chinese cities differs significantly from developed countries, with violation rates reaching 66.7% in some sections. Existing micro-simulation tools developed abroad fail to accurately represent Chinese traffic conditions due to the mixed traffic flow characteristics and high pedestrian violation rates.
method_summary: A pedestrian model for traffic system micro-simulation in China that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). The model uses questionnaires to determine proportions of pedestrian types under different circumstances and videotape analysis to extract time gap (DT) distributions for gap acceptance criteria. Pedestrians decide whether to violate traffic signals based on external factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle flow. Parameters are calibrated and validated against field data from Xi'an.
method_family: Agent-based microscopic traffic simulation / Behavioral modeling
tasks: Pedestrian road-crossing behavior simulation; Red light running prediction; Gap acceptance modeling
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students, 300 pedestrians); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples of DT)
metrics: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Pedestrian arriving interval distribution
claim_text: The model represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities
limitations: Current model only models pedestrian decision making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles; does not account for vehicle state changes (acceleration, deceleration) | DT calculation method needs improvement for turning vehicles to make model more practical | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians; self-reported behavior may not reflect actual daily behavior | Model calibrated specifically for Xi'an may not generalize well to other Chinese cities with different traffic characteristics
notes: Published in Transportation Research Part A, 2006; Study conducted in Xi'an, China; Road Traffic Safety Law implemented May 1st 2004 in China provided context for changing violation rates; Key parameters: r1 (proportion of law-obeying pedestrians), r2 (proportion of Type I who follow violators), r3 (proportion accepting specific DT); Pedestrian average walking speed used: 1.2 m/s

title: Untitled
research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), and extend the theory by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions
method_summary: Survey-based questionnaire study using structural equation modeling (SEM). A scenario-based questionnaire about crossing against pedestrian traffic lights was designed and administered in Dalian, China. Based on 260 complete and valid responses, reliability (Cronbach's alpha) and validity (confirmatory factor analysis) of data were evaluated. Four SEM models were tested to compare the predictive utility of basic TPB components versus extended models with additional factors.
tasks: predicting_pedestrian_violating_behavior_intention
datasets: Survey data from Dalian, China (n=260 respondents)
metrics: CMIN/df; RMSEA; P-close; CFI; regression_weights; Cronbach's_alpha
claim_text: Instrumental attitude (perceived benefits like saving time or convenience) is a significant predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention across all four models tested
limitations: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors like traffic violations; actual behavior was not observed for comparison or validation | Relatively small number of items per construct to maintain high internal reliability, which could affect the results | Perceived risk was not a significant predictor, suggesting pedestrians may not adequately perceive the susceptibility and seriousness of risk from traffic violations
notes: Study conducted in Dalian, China - a context where 40% of travel is on foot with common traffic violations; Model 4 (with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best fit (CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030); Sample: 57.3% female, 49.2% aged 18-24, 58.5% with undergraduate degree; Supported by Fundamental Research Funds for Central Universities (DUT14RW132) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (51308092)

title: Untitled
research_problem: Investigates how mobile phone distractions (music, phone conversation, and text) affect pedestrian crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection
method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment with 28 college students using two HD video cameras to record crossing behavior and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker to record visual attention. Compared three mobile phone distraction conditions (music listening, phone conversation with mental arithmetic, text messaging) against undistracted baseline.
tasks: pedestrian_crossing; mobile_phone_distraction
datasets: 28 college student participants (17 male, 11 female, ages 17-25)
metrics: initiate_time; look_left_right_frequency; crossing_speed; pupil_diameter; scanning_frequency; fixation_points; fixation_time; fixation_duration
claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction
limitations: Small sample size (n=28) limits generalizability to overall pedestrian population | Sample limited to college students (ages 17-25), may not represent other age groups | Data collection limited to one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume (fewer than 5 vehicles per minute) | Participants used unfamiliar experimental phone (Huawei P9) rather than their own devices, may not reflect real-world behavior | Mental arithmetic tasks for conversation and texting may not exactly conform to participants' daily mobile phone use patterns | Participants were restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation during experiment
notes: Published in Accident Analysis and Prevention (Volume 115, 2018, Pages 170-177); Experiment conducted December 28, 2016 to January 10, 2017; Used Tobii Pro Glasses 2 wearable eye-tracker at 50-HZ; 14-meter-wide crosswalk with 21-second green light and 39-second red phase; 40-km/h speed limit at experimental intersection

title: Untitled
research_problem: Identifying factors affecting pedestrian red light running propensity at signalized urban intersections in Hong Kong, where 62% of road fatalities are pedestrians and non-compliance with traffic signals constitutes about 25% of pedestrian crashes at signalized intersections.
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during peak and non-peak periods. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal phases across 652 cycles were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to examine both individual-level (pedestrian demographics and behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors affecting red light running propensity, while accounting for unobserved heterogeneity.
tasks: examine_effects_of_personal_factors_on_red_light_running; examine_effects_of_environmental_factors_on_red_light_running; examine_interaction_effects_between_personal_and_environmental_factors; model_individual_decision_of_red_light_running_violation; account_for_unobserved_heterogeneity_in_pedestrian_decision
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong; 652 signal cycles captured; Sites were pedestrian crash hot spots (each with >10 crashes in preceding 5 years)
metrics: red_light_running_violation_binary_outcome; gender; age_group_adolescent_adult_elderly; presence_of_companion; number_of_pedestrians_waiting; presence_of_other_violators; time_to_green; red_time; number_of_lanes; traffic_volume; percentage_of_heavy_vehicles
claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (b=-0.433, odds ratio=0.667), with 64.3% probability that females have lower propensity than males
limitations: The study focuses on propensity of red light running but does not assess safety outcomes (associated crash risk) - this is explicitly stated as a limitation | Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior is not measured despite being correlated with crash risk and severity | Data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and detailed socioeconomic characteristics (beyond age and gender) are not available from observation surveys, limiting understanding of decision-making processes | Sites limited to urban Hong Kong with specific traffic signal system (no pedestrian countdown display), limiting generalizability to other contexts with different signal configurations

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian street-crossing behavior at signalized intersections and developing a statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times during the red-man (red light) phase, which is important for traffic-light control and roadway layout design.
method_summary: The paper develops a bounded Pareto distribution model for pedestrian waiting times, incorporating vehicle time headway and pedestrian risk preferences (risk-taking vs risk-averse). Starting from a simplified scenario for a specific pedestrian under specific traffic conditions, the model uses statistical unconditioning to derive a mixture distribution with four components. The EM algorithm is employed for parameter estimation.
method_family: Statistical modeling, mixture distribution model, bounded Pareto distribution, generalized linear model, EM algorithm
tasks: Modeling pedestrian intended waiting times at signalized intersections; Analyzing pedestrian crossing behavior during red-man phase; Goodness-of-fit testing
datasets: Field data collected at a signalized intersection in Kunming, China - 283 valid observations collected on two consecutive weekend days (10:00 a.m. to 12:00 noon and 2:00 p.m. to 4:00 p.m.), with red-man phase duration of 75 seconds
metrics: Chi-square goodness-of-fit test; BIC for model selection
claim_text: The proposed model produces a U-shaped distribution for pedestrian intended waiting times at signalized intersections, showing that there are both many pedestrians who cross immediately after arriving and many who wait for the entire red-man phase
limitations: The paper focuses on univariate analysis only - only one variable (pedestrian waiting time) is explicitly modeled, and multivariate approaches are needed to simultaneously analyze multiple risk factors | Most existing multivariate approaches assume normal or exponential distributions which are not suitable for the bounded waiting time problem | The model requires sufficient data for each component in the mixture distribution, which may be challenging in practice
notes: Published in Transportation Research Part B (2013). The model has important implications for traffic-light control optimization and pedestrian safety. Data showed 21.9% pedestrian fatality rate in UK road accidents (2010) and 40-50% in Delhi. Model was applied to field data from Kunming, China with 283 observations.

datasets: Field data collected at a signalized intersection in Kunming, China - 283 valid observations collected on two consecutive weekend days (10:00 a.m. to 12:00 noon and 2:00 p.m. to 4:00 p.m.), with red-man phase duration of 75 seconds
metrics: Chi-square goodness-of-fit test; BIC for model selection
claim_text: Young pedestrians tend to be risk-takers while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse - with r1 (immediate crossing) decreasing with age and r4 (waiting entire phase) increasing with age
limitations: The paper focuses on univariate analysis only - only one variable (pedestrian waiting time) is explicitly modeled, and multivariate approaches are needed to simultaneously analyze multiple risk factors | Most existing multivariate approaches assume normal or exponential distributions which are not suitable for the bounded waiting time problem | The model requires sufficient data for each component in the mixture distribution, which may be challenging in practice
notes: Published in Transportation Research Part B (2013). The model has important implications for traffic-light control optimization and pedestrian safety. Data showed 21.9% pedestrian fatality rate in UK road accidents (2010) and 40-50% in Delhi. Model was applied to field data from Kunming, China with 283 observations.

title: Untitled
research_problem: The study investigates factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China. Pedestrian red-light running is identified as one of the main causes of pedestrian-involved traffic crashes at intersections, with over 127,000 pedestrians running red-lights in Shenzhen alone during the first half of 2015.
method_summary: A binomial logistic regression model was developed using survey data collected from Hefei, China. The questionnaire contained 15 items capturing pedestrian socioeconomic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples, the model predicts the probability of pedestrian red-light running behavior using significant explanatory variables.
tasks: Identify factors significantly affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior; Predict probability of red-light running at intersections; Compare results between China and other countries
datasets: 631 valid survey samples from Hefei, China (2015); Survey questionnaire with 15 items covering individual characteristics, trip characteristics, and pedestrian attitudes; Six signalized intersections selected near central business district or transit stations
metrics: Score test for variable significance (95% confidence level); Odds ratios; P-values
claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards road facility quality affecting crossing behavior
limitations: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei), limiting generalizability to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study only focused on signalized intersections, excluding other crossing facility types such as midblock crossings | Analysis focused on individual behavior, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and group behavior effects | Sample may not be fully representative as survey was conducted only during peak periods (May-June 2015)
notes: Study uses binomial logistic regression with Score test for variable selection; Four significant predictors identified: TRP2 (stroll trip purpose), TIS1/TIS2/TIS3 (time periods), INH (hurry attitude), IMR (road facility attitude); Odds ratios reported range from 0.299 to 3.326; Paper contributes to understanding pedestrian behavior differences in Chinese context compared to Western countries

title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding and modeling the interaction between vehicle yielding (VY) and pedestrian gap acceptance (PGA) behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, and analyzing their effects on traffic flow
method_summary: A microscopic traffic flow model is established by converging probabilistic models of vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance into a car-following model (based on the Full Velocity Difference model). The VY behavior uses a logit model with factors including gap size, number of lanes, number of waiting pedestrians, median location, and e-police presence. The PGA behavior uses a logit model with gap size, crossing distance, waiting time, and pedestrian position. The model is implemented in MATLAB time-step simulation (0.1s step) and validated against empirical data from two crosswalks in Shanghai.
method_family: Microscopic traffic flow model / Car-following model with integrated behavioral logit models
tasks: Vehicle-pedestrian interaction modeling; Traffic flow analysis at unsignalized midblock crosswalks; Sensitivity analysis of traffic and geometric factors; Recommendations for control mode selection (unsignalized vs signalized)
datasets: Empirical data from 295 Zhenhua Rd. crosswalk in Shanghai (313 veh/h/ln, 251 ped/h); Empirical data from 1565 Lingshi Rd. crosswalk in Shanghai (406 veh/h/ln, 172 ped/h); VY and PGA model parameters from 13 midblock crosswalk locations in Shanghai (Zhao et al., 2019; Malenje et al., 2019)
metrics: Average vehicular delay (seconds); Average pedestrian waiting time (seconds); Relative error between model and survey results; Wilcoxon signed-rank test p-value
claim_text: The proposed integrated model can effectively describe the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
limitations: The VY and PGA model parameters from Shanghai were used without recalibration, which may limit generalizability to other regions | The study only considers legal pedestrian crossing at designated crosswalks; illegal crossing behavior at unmarked locations is not addressed | The model assumes simplified arrival distributions (Poisson) which may not reflect real-world traffic fluctuations | The analysis does not consider the combined effect of adjacent intersections on traffic flow
notes: The study uses parameters j=0.41, k=0.2, V1=6.75, V2=7.91, C1=0.13, C2=1.57, lc=9 for the car-following model. The analysis period of 7200s (2 hours) was found to provide stable results. The VY utility function: Zn = 1.291 + 2.937Gn - 8.462Nl + 0.730Np + 3.008S + 11.913EP. The PGA utility function: Uil = 6.365 + 2.678Gl - 2.846D + 0.058Wi - 1.273Si.

title: Untitled
research_problem: Modeling pedestrian crossing speed profiles at signalized intersections, specifically addressing sudden speed changes (acceleration/deceleration) that cannot be anticipated by conflicting vehicles, which may lead to hazardous situations and pedestrian-vehicle conflicts
method_summary: A probabilistic discrete choice model determines acceleration/deceleration timing based on necessary speed to complete crossing (Vnec-Vcur), conflict area presence, and pedestrian flashing green (PFG) phase. Regression models (linear for acceleration, normal distribution for deceleration) calculate speed change amounts. Monte Carlo simulation generates entire speed profiles. Stepwise speed functions represent pedestrian speed profiles.
tasks: pedestrian_speed_change_prediction; pedestrian_vehicle_conflict_simulation; safety_assessment_at_signalized_intersections
datasets: Video data from 5 crosswalks at 3 intersections in Nagoya, Japan: Kanayama (East, North), Ueda (East, South), Fushimi (South); collected using TrafficAnalyzer image processing system at 0.5s intervals
metrics: modified_rho_squared; log_likelihood; crossing_time_distribution; vehicle_yield_rate; post_encroachment_time_PET
claim_text: The proposed model represents pedestrian travel time distribution more accurately than the constant speed model
limitations: The model underestimates probability of acceleration events (43.8% error in detecting actual acceleration events) | Pedestrian-pedestrian interaction not considered due to low traffic volumes at study sites | Pedestrian characteristics (age, gender, distraction) not incorporated in the model | Limited sample size from only five sites; some expected significant variables (PFG/PG length, crosswalk length) not found significant | Manual tracking using video processing may lead to tracking errors (measurement errors 0.14-0.38m) | Model does not consider real interaction between pedestrians and turning vehicles, only conflict area presence
notes: Published in Accident Analysis and Prevention (2017), Volume 108, Pages 332-342. Model integrates with existing vehicle maneuver models (Alhajyaseen et al.) for conflict simulation. Key application areas: traffic simulation for safety assessment, real-time information provision for vehicles, autonomous vehicle avoidance maneuver modeling. Data collected under uncongested/low-demand conditions to minimize pedestrian interactions.
